assessor_raw:
BASELINE ASSESSMENT — real_world_framing_009

The A/B test result is clean and the CTR lift of 2.1% (p < 0.001, tight CI) is real. The two-tower architecture and offline metrics support the finding. The model is technically superior to the incumbent.

However, the proposed full production rollout immediately is premature for two reasons.

First, the concentration effect creates contractual risk. The new model serves 73% of impressions from 8% of the catalog, compared to 55% under the incumbent. Items ranked 500th or lower receive 40% fewer impressions. The company has merchant agreements that guarantee minimum impression shares per quarter for contracted merchants. A 40% reduction in impressions for long-tail items may breach these contractual guarantees. This is not speculative — the data shows the concentration is happening, and the contracts create a legal obligation. The company must audit all contracted merchants' expected impression shares under the new model before proceeding to full deployment.

Second, the long-tail suppression has catalog health implications: product discovery for new and niche items, new merchant ramp-up velocity, and long-term catalog diversity all depend on some carousel presence. A 2-week CTR test does not capture these compounding effects.

Verdict: The CTR improvement is justified and the model is superior. Full rollout should proceed after (1) a merchant impression guarantee compliance audit clears contractual risk, and (2) a constrained ranking approach is implemented if the audit finds compliance failures.
